mnemonics.title: Python Indentation Rule
mnemonics.content: IP4: Indentation = 4 Spaces for code blocks - Consistent indentation is mandatory for proper execution.

mnemonics.title: Program Structure Order
mnemonics.content: DIA G M M: Docstring → Imports → Assignments → Functions → Main block - Standard Python program layout.

mnemonics.title: Comment Types
mnemonics.content: #H for Hash (single-line), '''T''' for Triple quotes (multi-line) - Two ways to add comments in Python.

acronyms.usage: Documents that describe Python's features, styles, and guidelines like PEP 8 (style guide) and PEP 20 (Zen of Python).
The default IDE that comes with Python for writing and executing code.
An interactive environment that reads commands, evaluates them, prints results, and loops - used in Python shell.
An environment variable that tells Python where to find modules to import.

visualTips.visualization: Think of indentation like staircase steps - each level represents being inside a loop, function, or conditional. Higher step = deeper inside the block.
Imagine __name__ as a name tag. When script runs directly, it's called "__main__" (the main person). When imported, it keeps its module name (like a visitor with their own ID).
Top-to-bottom flow like reading a book - Python reads line 1, then line 2, etc., jumping only when it hits functions or loops.

analogies.analogy: Like a translator in a meeting - Python interpreter translates your English-like code into machine language on-the-fly, line by line.
Like indentation in an outline - bullet points nested under a main point show they belong to that section. Python uses this for code too.
Like chapters in a book - each module is a separate file (chapter) that can be referenced and used in the main program (the whole book).
